topic: Why the Internet Wasn't Designed for Security
title: How the Internet Grew Before Security Was the Priority

lesson.learn:
The internet was designed first for connectivity, not for trust. Early network designers wanted computers to communicate reliably across long distances, so the focus was on sharing data and keeping it moving even if parts of the network failed. Security came later, after the network had already grown into something much larger and more public.

That is why so many internet systems had to be patched with encryption, authentication, and firewalls after the fact. The original architecture assumed a far more trusted environment than the modern web actually has.
lesson.simpler: The internet was built to connect machines, not to lock them down. Security had to be added later.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Security was not the starting goal of the internet, so later layers had to make up the gap.
lesson.deeperDive:
Packet switching and the end-to-end design philosophy made the network flexible and resilient, but also left many protocols open by default. Early services such as email and file transfer often traveled in plain text, which meant anyone in the middle could potentially read them. Over time, tools like TLS, VPNs, firewalls, and secure authentication became essential patches on top of the original design.

That history explains why internet security is still an ongoing battle. The network was not broken at the beginning; it was simply built for a different world.
lesson.funFact: Early internet traffic often traveled with no encryption at all.
references: IETF RFC archive - internet protocol standards
NIST - TLS and network security guidance

people: Bob Kahn - key ARPANET and TCP/IP architect
J. C. R. Licklider - early networking visionary
places: Stanford Research Institute - early internet node
Network security teams - later defenders of the open web
concepts: Packet switching - breaking data into smaller pieces
